coredata.dc:title: Surface plasmon-induced photodegradation of methylene blue with single layer graphene quantum dots/Au nanospheres under visible-light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
Bayat, Amir
Ardekani, Saeed Rahemi
coredata.dc:description: Single-layer graphene quantum dots and gold (SLGQDs/Au) nanospheres composite was synthesized by hydrothermal and photochemical methods. Single-layer graphene quantum dot was synthesized by glucose precursor via hydrothermal method and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite was prepared by adding HAuCl4 under UV irradiation. Characterization of sample was performed using XRD, Raman, FESEM, UV–vis spectroscopy and PL analysis. The quantum yield of SLGQDs/Au composite was calculated. The role of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of gold nanoparticles in quantum yield reduction and the effect of quantum yield on photodegradation was discussed. Using the optimal sample, a photocatalyst test was performed on methylene blue, which near 90% photocatalytic degradation was achieved under visible light irradiation in 120 min. Compared to bare GQD the high photocatalytic efficiency of composite could be due to the presence of SPR level of Au nanoparticles between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of SLGQDs. Under light irradiation, SLGQDs and Au nanoparticles absorb visible light energy. By absorbing the photons, the electrons of SLGQDS could be excited to LUMO band and leave holes in HOMO band. Spontaneously the emitted photons excite the Fermi level electrons of Au nanoparticles to the SPR state. Created SPR level can suppress electron-holes recombination. Then the photo-exited electrons react with dissolved oxygen to generate ° O2 − and ° OH radicals meanwhile, the holes could oxide OH− into OH°. Created radicals which are strong oxidants degrade the pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single layer graphene quantum dot
Surface plasmon resonance
Quantum yield
Photo degradation
Photochemical
UV irradiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis mechanism of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres under UV illumination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the hydrothermal synthesis of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres.
(a) AFM image and (b) height profile and (c) TEM image of SLGQDs.
(a) and (b) FESEM images, and (c) and (d) TEM images of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres.
Raman spectra of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanospheres.
XRD patterns of (a) SLGQDs and (b) SLGQDs/Au nanospheres and (c) EDX profile.
(a) Absorbance spectra and (b) PL emission spectra of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanospheres.
UV–Vis spectra of degraded methylene blue with optimized sample (SLGQDs/Au85) under visible light irradiation.
Proposed mechanism for photodegradation of MB using SLGQDS/Au nanospheres.
Quantum yield of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au85 nanospheres.

head.ce:title: Surface plasmon-induced photodegradation of methylene blue with single layer graphene quantum dots/Au nanospheres under visible-light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-layer graphene quantum dots and gold (SLGQDs/Au) nanospheres composite was synthesized by hydrothermal and photochemical methods. Single-layer graphene quantum dot was synthesized by glucose precursor via hydrothermal method and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite was prepared by adding HAuCl4 under UV irradiation. Characterization of sample was performed using XRD, Raman, FESEM, UV–vis spectroscopy and PL analysis. The quantum yield of SLGQDs/Au composite was calculated. The role of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of gold nanoparticles in quantum yield reduction and the effect of quantum yield on photodegradation was discussed. Using the optimal sample, a photocatalyst test was performed on methylene blue, which near 90% photocatalytic degradation was achieved under visible light irradiation in 120 min. Compared to bare GQD the high photocatalytic efficiency of composite could be due to the presence of SPR level of Au nanoparticles between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of SLGQDs. Under light irradiation, SLGQDs and Au nanoparticles absorb visible light energy. By absorbing the photons, the electrons of SLGQDS could be excited to LUMO band and leave holes in HOMO band. Spontaneously the emitted photons excite the Fermi level electrons of Au nanoparticles to the SPR state. Created SPR level can suppress electron-holes recombination. Then the photo-exited electrons react with dissolved oxygen to generate ° O2 − and ° OH radicals meanwhile, the holes could oxide OH− into OH°. Created radicals which are strong oxidants degrade the pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials with high photocatalytic activity have found great role in new researches with increasing use of photocatalysts in various fields such as wastewater treatment, water splitting andsolar cells [2–5][1]. Synthesis of photocatalytic compounds with the ability to be used under visible light has attracted special attention in recent years [6–9]. Due to their abundance, ease of construction, low cost, stability and coverage of a wide range of electrical and optical properties, Carbon structures such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, graphene quantum dots, etc. have played a significant role in technologies related to energy and the environment [10–12]. Among these, zero dimensional graphene quantum dots have attracted more attention in photocatalytic technologies, because of their non-toxicity, chemical and physical stability, low cost, changeable band gap and good photocatalytical activity. Optical properties of GQD such as optimizing the absorption and emission of photons, band gap, HOMO and LUMO bands have important role in many of these applications [13–17]. Recent researches have focused on composing graphene quantum dot with metals such as gold, silver, palladium, and platinum due to their special nanometer size and light absorption to optimize the optical behavior and increase its photocatalytic activity. In these metals oscillating of free electrons with high concentration that is generated by light irradiation on the surface of metal nanoparticles named surface plasmon resonance (SPR) has the fundamental role to optimize the optical properties of their composites with other fluorophores such as GQDs [18–23].

Despite its high price, gold metal is a good option to compose with graphene quantum dot due to its low oxidation and good physical and chemical stability [24–26]. Composite construction of graphene quantum dot and metals could improve the photocatalytic activity of the material by exciting more electrons and improving charge separation due to SPR. Choosing the suitable approach to synthesis nanomaterials and also their composition is important to achieve the best possible result at the lowest possible cost. Various routs such as hydrothermal [26–28], solvothermal [29,30], pyrolysis [31,32], laser ablation [33,34] and photochemical [35–37] have been used to prepare graphene quantum dot and its composite with metal nanoparticles.

Hydrothermal is a low cost, simple and controllable method to synthesis quantum dots. For making composite of graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticles, photochemical method could be appropriate due to its simple, affordable and exciting performance in formation of bond between GQDs and Au nanoparticles. Many researches have been done by scientists in recent years to develop this field [35,38–41]. Park, et al. reported synthesis of GQD/Au nanocomposite for optical investigation [39], Anh et al. reported preparation of S doped graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles for sensing application [41] and Also, for the photocatalytic application of carbon compounds quantum dot/ gold particle, significant works have already been done, Which can be referred to the synthesis of N and S doped carbon dots and Au nanoparticles composite by Mahto et al., in situ decoration of plasmonic Au nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots-graphitic carbon nitride hybrid by G Rajender, et al. and synthesis of gold carbon dot core shells by Mehta et al. [40,42,43].

Herein, we have synthesized novel single layer GQDs (SLGQDs)/Au nanospheres with concentration on optimization of light absorption and PL emissions by using of the metal nanoparticles SPR. SLGQD/Au nanospheres were synthesized by a facile photochemical route with the simultaneous reduction of GQDs and formation of Au nanoparticles. The morphology of the synthesized nanocomposite as well as its optical and physical properties are investigated. The as-prepared nanocomposite shows appropriate photocatalytic performance in degradation of methylene blue (MB) solution.
All materials and chemical reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich without any further purification.

In order to synthesis of SLGQDs, 0.03 g glucose (CAS: 50-99-7) dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and stirred in room temperature for 15 min. The colorless precursor solution heated in 180 °C for 8 h. The color of heated solution was altered to light brown after synthesis process. The resulting sample was dialyzed by dialysis bags with cut-off molecular weight (Mw) of 3500 Da to remove oversized particles. Schematic illustration of SLGQDs formation is shown in Fig. 1.

To hybridize SLGQDs and Au, 1.5 mM solution of HAuCl4 (CAS: 16961-25-4) was added into the SLGQD solution. The SLGQD/Au nanospheres composite was prepared by different quantities of HAuCl4 solution (20,45 and 85 μL), and a constant molar concentration of SLGQD particles (1 mL). For photo- chemical synthesis (Fig. 1), the solution mixture was irradiated under UV light emitted by a 16 W power source for 2 h to form the SLGQD/Au nanospheres. Dissociation of HAuCl4 to Au3+, H+ and Cl– radicals and excitation of electrons in the SLGQDs are the result of UV photons. Higher reduction potential of Au3+ than the GQDs leads to the formation of SLGQD/Au nanoparticles. The SLGQD/Au nanospheres are formed by aggregation and self-assembly via hydrogen bonding aurophilicity interactions. Optimum pH to reach nanosphere morphology for this composite was about 7–8. Eventually after the formation of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres, a light purple solution is created. Because of focus on the optical properties of the SLGQDs/Au structure, analyzes of optically optimized sample (SLGQDs/Au85) presented in the following.

Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis was performed by a MIRA3 TESCAN instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Philips Expert instrument with Cu kα radiation. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) micrographs were taken by a Veeco Autoprobe CP research microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out by a CM30 300 kV instrument. UV–Vis spectra were obtained by a Perkin-Elmer lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Emissions of Photoluminescence (PL) were assessed using a PL spectrometer of Gilden photonics. Raman spectroscopy was carried out with 532 nm excitation by Raman spectrometer (Takram P50C0R10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis mechanism of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres under UV illumination
ce:section.ce:para: To break the bonds between the elements of HAuCl4, ultraviolet light with an appropriate frequency was irradiated to the solution, gold salt is broken down into H+, Cl- and Au3+, and on the other hand, the electrons of the GQDs are excited. Au nanoparticles will be formed by attaching separated electrons to Au3+. In the last step by forming aurophilic interactions between Au nanoparticles and hydrogen bond between graphene quantum dots, spherical nanostructures are formed. The synthesis process of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres is shown in Fig. 1.

AFM analysis was used to estimate the thickness of the obtained SLGQDs, the height profile and AFM image of SLGQDs are shown in Fig. 2a and b. According to the figure as regards mica has been selected as substrate with a very smooth surface and the measurement error of the AFM height profile is 0.1 nm [44], it is clear that the height of SLGQDs is less than 0.5 nm which confirms that the synthesized GQDs are single-layered. TEM image of SLGQDs is also shown in Fig. 2c. Based on this image, which shows the uniform distribution of particles, the size of the SLGQDs is about 5 nanometers. The inset in Fig. 2c shows the typical HRTEM image of the SLGQDs, with the lattice parameter of 0.35 nm.

The FESEM image of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres is shown in Fig. 3a and b. The size of the SLGQD/Au composite is in the range of 0.8–3 µm with a mean size of 2 µm. TEM image of the composition is shown in Fig. 3c and d. The accumulation of graphene quantum dots around the gold is evident in the TEM images. By combining a number of these structures, the spheres are formed that was shown in the FESEM images. The uniformity of synthesized particles are completely obvious in both TEM and FESEM images.

Fig. 4 indicates the Raman shifts spectra of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au. Raman modes of SLGQDs centered at 1340 and 1615 cm−1 conﬁrmed the existence of the graphitic sp2 carbon domain along with sp3 carbon atoms in SLGQDs. These modes are associated with the disordered (D) band, and crystalline (G) band respectively. After formation of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres, the D and G band peaks shifted to 1398 and 1587 cm−1 respectively [45–47]. This shift in the Raman peaks of the GQD/Au nanostructure could be due to the charge transfer from the GQDs to Au, which enhances the electron–phonon coupling [47–49]. The intensity ratio of the disordered peak (ID) and graphitic peak (IG) for SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites were about 1.33 and 0.82 respectively. The intensity ratio between the D and G bands (ID/IG) represents the degree of disorder and the sp2 domain size in graphene-based composites [49]. The reduction of ID/IG ratio indicates that the level of disorder and impurity reduced after the hybridization of Au and GQDs. The graphene defects are reduced with the participation of free bands and oxygen in the composition process.

The XRD experiment was conducted to investigate the phase purity and successful formation of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres. In the XRD pattern of SLGQD that is shown in Fig. 5a, a wide peak is seen at about 27 ̊ that corresponds to graphitic structure of SLGQDs. As shown in Fig. 5b the broad peak at about 28 ̊ still exist that can be considered as graphitic peak of SLGQDs in SLGQDs/Au nanospheres pattern. Other peaks centered at 44.7° and 52.07° are devoted to the (111) and (200) planes of face-centered crystalline Au nanoparticles (JCPDS: 004-0784). As indicated in XRD pattern of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres, the crystallinity of SLGQDs and Au nanospheres was preserved and new morphologies was formed which can play an important role to improve the optical properties.

EDX analyze indicates the elemental composition of sample. As is shown in Fig. 5c, EDX profile of prepared SLGQDs/Au nanospheres indicates the existence of Au, C and O and their weight percentage in synthesized composite. The amount of Au and C in the nanocomposite was obtained 37.17 wt% and 34.11 wt%, respectively. No other elements were observed in the SLGGQD/Au nanocomposite that confirms the purity of synthesized nanocomposite.

Fig. 6a indicates the absorption spectra of the SLGQDs and SLGQD/Au nanospheres that was investigated by UV–visible spectra. There are two common peaks in both samples. The peak at 244 nm refers to sp2 hybridized structure resulted by CC and CC and the revealed peak at 305 nm in SLGQDs and 284 nm in SLGQDs/Au sample is attributed to sp3 hybridized structure resulted by the CO bond due to the n-π* transition [50,51]. The SPR effect of Au nanoparticles in SLGQDs/Au sample was revealed by a peak at 533 nm.

Fig. 6b shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanocomposites with different concentration of Au. The PL profile behavior indicates, as the concentration of gold increases, the amount of emitted photons decrease and leads to quench of the PL peak. Decrease in emission of photons is a sign of reducing the electron-hole recombination of graphene quantum dots. Retarding the recombination can create appropriate conditions for using in solar cells and photocatalytic processes that the second application is described in the next section. It is obvious from PL profiles that there are two peaks for SLGQD at 449 and 474 nm before adding HAuCl4, while the SLGQDs/Au samples show two peaks at about 440 and 475 nm. The intensity reduction in these peaks is obvious compared with PL peak of SLGQD. The quench of these peaks returns to the plasmon resonance of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres at 533 nm, which was revealed in the UV–Vis profile [36,50,52]. Gold nanoparticles cause the accumulation of GQDs around them, this accumulation reduces the distance between the GQDs, which is one of the factors in quenching the photoluminescence radiation of GQDs. On the other hand Gold nanoparticles act as a hotspot and increase the number of excited electrons. These electrons, in quenching conditions, according to the application, instead of recombining with holes and producing photons, participate in other processes such as increasing current, increasing free radicals, etc., which will be explained in the photocatalyst mechanism.

Quinine sulfate was used as a reference to determine the quantum yield of SLGQDs and SLGQDs/Au nanospheres (as seen in Table 1). It was calculated as following [16]:
(1) φ= φr (I/Ir)(n2/nr 2)(Ar/A)

where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density. The subscript “r” refers to the reference of known quantum yield. The PL quantum yields of SLGQDs and optically optimized sample (SLGQDs/Au85) is 9% and 0.9% respectively. Obvious decrease in quantum yield of SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite compared with SLGQDs confirms the reduction of recombination of electron-holes. More separate electrons and holes lead to better catalyst activity as they can form more free radicals.

The performance of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres was analyzed to degrade organic dye of methylene blue. The UV–Vis spectra of the degraded methylene blue is shown in Fig. 7. As regards the degradation profile of MB, it is obvious that the peak intensity of the MB characteristic absorbance at λ = 661 nm reduces with increasing the irradiation time, which shows the MB degradation by the SLGQDs/Au nanospheres. The degradation percent of methylene blue solution was calculated by the following equation [16]:
(2)
Degradation (%) =
C
0
-
C
t
C
0
× 100
where C0 and Ct are the methylene blue concentration at time 0 and t, respectively. about 85% of methylene blue was degraded under visible light irradiation after 120 min. The high degradation percent of MB under visible light is attributed to wide absorbance of GQDs/Au nanoparticles, presence of Au nanoparticles as nanoantennas to create hotspots for absorbing more photons and also existing of SPR level to prevent electron-holes recombination.

Fig. 8 indicates the photocatalytic mechanism of SLGQDs/Au nanospheres. The SLGQDs with large surface area are able to connect Methylene blue molecules. More interaction of MB with the nanophotocatalyst compounds, cause more photodegradation efficiency. With the light irradiation, Au nanoparticles act as nanoantennas in the composite to concentrate energy of photons. This energy consumes to excite more electrons. The LUMO band electrons of MB proceed to the LUMO band of SLGQDs. Also the holes move in opposite position in HOMO bands of MB and SLGQDs. This activity could results in degradation of Methylene Blue by trapping its electrons. In another manner, the electrons react with the dissolved oxygen and generate active superoxide radicals and the holes are trapped by the hydroxyl ions at the photocatalyst surface to produce reactive hydroxyl radicals (OH°). The generated reactive radicals react with Methylene Blue adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface and degrade the organic dye molecules. On the other hand, the accumulation of quantum dots around gold particles and their very close distance to each other, causes the formation of intermediate levels and reduces the recombination of conduction band electrons and capacitance band holes. Therefore, the transfer of surface plasmon resonance electrons to the conduction band of quantum dots can also help increase the radical-forming electrons and ultimately increase the degradation of methylene blue particles [44,53,54].

Low cost, gram scale single layer graphene quantum dots (SLGQDs) with less than four angstrom height, were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Glucose was the only precursor used in this method. In the next step, single layer graphene quantum dots and Au nanospheres were formed by photochemical method. By performing a UV test, the presence of the gold absorption peak was determined and after examining the different concentrations, the optimum amount to reach the lowest intensity of emission peak was determined by PL test. As expected, the optimal sample of SLGQDs/Au nanocomposite had a quantum efficiency equivalent to less than one tenth of SLGQDs. Photocatalytic activity of SLGQDs/Au nanosphere was evaluated by degradation of MB under visible light irradiation. The role of large surface area of SLGQDs to attach more MB molecules and also the effect of Au nanoparticles and its SPR to form hotspots and increase the radical-forming electrons in the photodegradation process was discussed.

Mohamad Taghi Dejpasand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Supervision. Amir Bayat: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Saeed Rahemi Ardekani: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Research Council of the Tarbiat Modares University for financial supports.
